name: Build Budget
description: turns income and bills into a workable monthly spending plan
when_to_use: when the user wants to make a budget or get their monthly money under control
body:
1. ask for take-home income and a list of fixed bills (rent, utilities, loans, insurance).
2. subtract fixed costs from income to get what's left for variable spend + saving.
3. split the leftover into buckets (groceries, transport, fun, savings) using their priorities, not a generic rule.
4. set one concrete dollar cap per bucket and name where overflow gets pulled from.
5. end with the single number they must not exceed in discretionary spend this month.

name: Expense Review
description: audits recent transactions to find where money actually went
when_to_use: when the user wants to understand or cut their spending after seeing statements
body:
1. ask them to paste or describe the last 30-60 days of transactions.
2. bucket every line into categories; flag anything uncategorizable for them to confirm.
3. total each category and rank biggest to smallest.
4. call out the top 3 categories and any single charges that look off or surprising.
5. propose two specific cuts with the dollar amount each would save per month.

name: Savings Plan
description: works backward from a goal to a required monthly set-aside
when_to_use: when the user has a target amount or purchase and wants to save for it
body:
1. get the goal amount, the deadline, and what they already have saved toward it.
2. compute the gap and divide by months remaining to get the raw monthly number.
3. sanity-check that number against their free cash flow; if it doesn't fit, adjust deadline or amount.
4. pick where the money lives (separate account, HYSA) and whether to automate the transfer.
5. end with the exact monthly amount and the date they hit the goal.

name: Compare Offers
description: puts two financial offers side by side on a common basis
when_to_use: when the user is choosing between two loans, cards, jobs, plans, or quotes
body:
1. list both offers and pull every cost, rate, fee, and perk for each.
2. normalize them to the same time window and units (annual cost, effective rate, total over term).
3. build a two-column table so differences are obvious at a glance.
4. note any non-money factors that matter (flexibility, penalties, lock-in).
5. state which one wins for their situation and the one tradeoff they're accepting.

name: Subscription Audit
description: finds and ranks recurring charges worth cutting
when_to_use: when the user suspects they're paying for things they don't use
body:
1. scan statements for recurring or near-monthly charges, including annual ones split out.
2. list each with its price, billing cycle, and last time they used it if known.
3. sort into keep, downgrade, and cancel based on actual use.
4. total the annual cost of the cancel pile so the savings feel real.
5. give the exact cancel steps or links for the top 2-3.

name: Explain Fee
description: decodes a confusing charge and whether it can be avoided
when_to_use: when the user sees a fee or charge they don't understand
body:
1. get the exact label, amount, and where it appeared.
2. identify what it is in plain terms and what triggered it.
3. say whether it's a one-time, recurring, or conditional fee.
4. explain the specific action that avoids or waives it next time.
5. tell them if it's worth disputing and how, or if it's just the cost of business.

name: Debt Payoff
description: orders debts and sets a payoff sequence and timeline
when_to_use: when the user has multiple debts and wants a plan to clear them
body:
1. list every debt with balance, rate, and minimum payment.
2. lock in all minimums first so nothing goes delinquent.
3. pick a method: avalanche (highest rate first, saves most) or snowball (smallest first, faster wins) and say why.
4. apply every spare dollar to the target debt, roll its payment to the next once cleared.
5. give the order, the monthly extra needed, and the rough debt-free date.

name: Emergency Fund
description: sizes and stages a cash cushion for unexpected costs
when_to_use: when the user wants a safety net or asks how much cash to keep on hand
body:
1. add up bare-minimum monthly costs (housing, food, utilities, insurance, minimum debt).
2. multiply by 3-6 months based on income stability and dependents.
3. compare to current liquid cash and find the gap.
4. set a starter milestone (one month) before the full target so it feels reachable.
5. name the account it sits in and the monthly contribution to fill it.

name: Big Purchase
description: pressure-tests a large buy before committing
when_to_use: when the user is about to spend a lot on one thing and wants a gut check
body:
1. get the price, the reason for buying, and the timing.
2. check it against current cash and whether it dents emergency savings or adds debt.
3. break down total cost of ownership, not just sticker (taxes, insurance, upkeep, financing interest).
4. weigh buy now vs wait-and-save vs cheaper alternative.
5. give a clear go / wait / skip call with the reason in one line.

name: Paycheck Split
description: allocates each paycheck across needs, wants, and goals on payday
when_to_use: when the user wants a repeatable rule for what to do when money lands
body:
1. get net pay per check and pay frequency.
2. carve off fixed obligations due before the next check first.
3. route fixed percentages to savings and debt before any spending happens (pay yourself first).
4. leave the remainder as spendable and convert it to a per-day or per-week number.
5. write it as a simple checklist they run every payday.

name: Cash Flow
description: maps timing of money in vs out to avoid running short
when_to_use: when the user keeps hitting low balances or overdrafts despite enough income
body:
1. lay income dates and amounts on a calendar.
2. place every bill and recurring charge on its due date.
3. walk the running balance day by day to find the danger dips.
4. shift movable due dates or build a small buffer to smooth the low points.
5. point to the exact dates to watch and the buffer amount that fixes them.

name: Price Per Use
description: compares options by what they cost each time you actually use them
when_to_use: when the user is deciding between buy vs rent, cheap vs durable, or membership vs pay-as-you-go
body:
1. get the total cost of each option including upkeep over its life.
2. estimate honest usage count over the same period.
3. divide cost by uses to get price per use for each.
4. flag the breakeven usage where the pricier option wins.
5. recommend based on their real usage, not the optimistic version.

name: Raise Math
description: translates a pay change into real take-home and lifestyle impact
when_to_use: when the user gets a raise, offer, or rate change and wants the actual net effect
body:
1. take the gross change and estimate taxes and any bracket shift.
2. subtract increased deductions (retirement %, benefits) that scale with pay.
3. show the net monthly increase, not the gross headline.
4. suggest splitting the new money before lifestyle absorbs it (save x, spend y).
5. end with the realistic extra dollars per month they'll actually see.

name: Net Worth
description: tallies assets minus debts into one tracked number
when_to_use: when the user wants a single snapshot of their financial position or to track progress
body:
1. list assets: cash, accounts, investments, property, anything resellable at honest value.
2. list liabilities: every loan, card balance, and amount owed.
3. subtract liabilities from assets for the net worth figure.
4. note the 1-2 line items moving it most so they know the levers.
5. set a cadence (monthly/quarterly) to re-run and compare the trend.

name: Tax Estimate
description: quickly sizes the tax bill or refund before filing season
when_to_use: when the user wants a rough sense of what they owe or will get back before they file
body:
1. get total gross income from all sources for the year.
2. ask about obvious deductions: retirement contributions, student loan interest, hsa, standard vs itemized.
3. estimate taxable income and apply the relevant federal brackets.
4. subtract withholding already paid to find owe or refund.
5. flag any surprise areas (side income, capital gains, underpayment penalty risk).
6. give the rough number and say whether to adjust withholding now.

name: 50/30/20 Check
description: scores current spend against the 50/30/20 rule and shows what's off
when_to_use: when the user wants a quick gut-check on whether their spending split is healthy
body:
1. get net monthly income and actual spend by category.
2. sort categories into needs (50%), wants (30%), savings/debt (20%).
3. calculate the percentage falling in each bucket vs the target.
4. highlight the one bucket most over and by how much in dollars.
5. suggest the single shift that brings the worst bucket back in range.

name: Credit Score
description: explains what's hurting or helping the score and what to fix first
when_to_use: when the user wants to understand or improve their credit score
body:
1. ask for the current score range and the top negative factors if they have a report.
2. map each factor to its weight: payment history 35%, utilization 30%, length 15%, mix/new 20%.
3. rank the factors by impact and ease of fix.
4. give one concrete action for the highest-impact fixable item (pay down card x to below 30% of limit, etc.).
5. set a realistic timeline for visible score movement.

name: Refi Math
description: calculates if refinancing a loan actually saves money after closing costs
when_to_use: when the user is considering refinancing a mortgage, auto loan, or student loan
body:
1. get current rate, remaining balance, remaining term, and monthly payment.
2. get the new rate, new term, and estimated closing costs or fees.
3. calculate new monthly payment and the monthly savings.
4. divide total closing costs by monthly savings to get the break-even month.
5. compare break-even to how long they plan to hold the loan - refi only wins if they stay past it.

name: Investment Basics
description: explains a financial product or term in plain language without jargon
when_to_use: when the user asks what something is (etf, bond, roth, index fund, etc.) and wants a plain explanation
body:
1. identify the exact term or product they asked about.
2. describe it in one sentence as if explaining to someone with no finance background.
3. say how it actually works mechanically (what you buy, how you earn, who holds it).
4. name the main risk and the main benefit.
5. give a concrete one-line example with real numbers if it helps.

name: Side Income
description: sizes up a side hustle's real after-cost, after-tax earnings
when_to_use: when the user is thinking about starting or evaluating a side gig or freelance income stream
body:
1. get the expected gross revenue and hours per month.
2. subtract direct costs (supplies, platform fees, mileage, tools).
3. estimate self-employment tax and income tax on the net profit.
4. compute true hourly rate after costs and taxes.
5. compare to their current job's hourly equivalent and flag whether it's worth the time.

name: Irregular Income
description: builds a stable spending system when income varies month to month
when_to_use: when the user is freelance, commission-based, or has unpredictable income
body:
1. find the lowest single-month income over the past year - that's the floor to budget from.
2. set fixed expenses to be coverable by the floor, not the average.
3. in good months, fill an income-smoothing buffer before spending anything extra.
4. define what counts as 'surplus' and route it: buffer first, then debt/savings, then discretionary.
5. give a simple rule: if buffer is full and bills are covered, the rest is free to spend.

name: Money Tradeoff
description: shows what else the money could do before committing it
when_to_use: when the user is about to make a large financial decision and should weigh alternatives
body:
1. note the amount being considered and its intended use.
2. list 2-3 realistic alternative uses: paying debt, investing, emergency fund, etc.
3. calculate the rough 5-year value of each alternative (debt interest saved, investment return at 7%).
4. contrast that with the value or enjoyment from the intended use.
5. present the tradeoff plainly and let them decide - don't moralize, just make the cost visible.

name: Windfall Plan
description: allocates an unexpected lump sum without wasting it
when_to_use: when the user receives unexpected money (bonus, tax refund, inheritance, gift) and wants to use it well
body:
1. get the amount and whether any tax is still owed on it.
2. lock in a split order: emergency fund gap → high-interest debt → savings goal → invest → spend.
3. ask if there's a specific goal the money was mentally earmarked for.
4. fill each bucket in order until the money runs out, stopping when it does.
5. give the final dollar breakdown across buckets and note anything left as guilt-free spend.

name: Insurance Check
description: reviews whether current coverage is adequate or over-bought
when_to_use: when the user wants to know if they have the right amount or type of insurance
body:
1. list the types they currently have and monthly or annual premiums.
2. for each, ask what the deductible and payout limit are.
3. flag obvious gaps (no renters/term life with dependents, no umbrella with assets) or clear over-coverage.
4. estimate whether the gap between premium savings and risk exposure is worth switching.
5. give 1-2 concrete changes: cancel x, add y, or raise deductible on z to drop premium by $n/mo.
